title: Docker Cheat Sheet
description: 48 Docker commands with flags, real-world use cases, and gotchas. Searchable and filterable. Verified against official Docker docs.

commands.title: Build an image

flags.description: Tag the built image with a name and optional tag, e.g. myapp:latest
Use a Dockerfile at a path other than ./Dockerfile

commands.useCase: Build a container image from a Dockerfile before pushing it to a registry or running it locally.
commands.beginnerNote: The dot at the end is the build context — it is not optional, and sending a large directory as context slows every build. Add a .dockerignore file to exclude node_modules and other large directories.

commands.title: List local images
commands.useCase: See every image pulled or built locally, along with size, to find what's eating disk space.

commands.title: Pull an image from a registry
commands.useCase: Download a specific image and tag before running it or as part of a CI pipeline warm-up step.

commands.title: Push an image to a registry
commands.useCase: Publish a locally built image to a registry like Docker Hub or a private ECR/GCR repository.

commands.title: Tag an image
commands.useCase: Give a locally built image the fully-qualified name a registry expects before pushing it.

commands.title: Remove an image
commands.useCase: Free up disk space by deleting an old or unused image tag.
commands.beginnerNote: Fails if any container, running or stopped, still references the image. Remove or prune the container first.

commands.title: Remove dangling images

flags.description: Remove all unused images, not just dangling (untagged) ones

commands.useCase: Clean up untagged layers left behind by repeated docker build runs during local development.
commands.beginnerNote: With -a this removes every image not currently used by a container, not just dangling ones — check docker ps -a first if you don't want to re-pull anything.

commands.title: Run a container

flags.description: Run detached in the background instead of attaching to the terminal
Allocate an interactive TTY and keep STDIN open, for shells and interactive tools
Publish a container port to a host port, host:container
Give the container a fixed, memorable name instead of a random one
Automatically remove the container when it exits

commands.useCase: Start a new container from an image, mapping ports and mounting volumes as needed for local development.
commands.beginnerNote: -d runs detached in the background, -it allocates an interactive terminal — they serve completely different purposes. Using -d then wondering why there is no output is the most common docker run confusion.

commands.title: List running containers

flags.description: Show all containers including stopped ones, not just running

commands.useCase: Check what's currently running before starting a duplicate container or debugging why a port is already in use.
commands.beginnerNote: Shows only running containers by default. Use docker ps -a to see stopped containers, including ones that exited with an error.

commands.title: List all containers including stopped
commands.useCase: Find a container that exited unexpectedly so you can inspect its exit code and logs.

commands.title: Start a stopped container
commands.useCase: Resume a previously stopped container with the same configuration instead of running a new one.

commands.title: Stop a running container
commands.useCase: Gracefully stop a container, sending SIGTERM and waiting before force-killing it, such as before a maintenance window.
commands.beginnerNote: Waits up to 10 seconds for graceful shutdown by default before sending SIGKILL. Use --time to change the grace period for slow-shutdown apps.

commands.title: Restart a container
commands.useCase: Pick up a changed environment variable or config file mount without recreating the container from scratch.

commands.title: Remove a container

flags.description: Force removal of a running container by stopping it first

commands.useCase: Delete a stopped container permanently, freeing its name so a new container can reuse it.
commands.beginnerNote: Fails on a running container unless you pass -f — this is a safety guard, not a bug.

commands.title: Create a container without starting it
commands.useCase: Pre-create a container's filesystem and config to inspect or modify before actually starting it.

commands.title: View container logs

flags.description: Stream logs continuously instead of printing a static snapshot
Only show the last N lines
Only show logs newer than a relative time or timestamp

commands.useCase: Read stdout and stderr from a running or recently stopped container to debug a startup failure.
commands.beginnerNote: Without -f it prints a snapshot and exits. With -f it streams, but the stream stops if the container restarts — you'll need to run it again.

commands.title: View live resource usage
commands.useCase: Watch live CPU, memory, and network usage across all running containers to spot a runaway process.

commands.title: View running processes in a container
commands.useCase: Check what processes are actually running inside a container without needing a shell inside it.

commands.title: Inspect a container's configuration
commands.useCase: Get the full JSON configuration of a container, including mounted volumes, network settings, and env vars, for deep debugging.

commands.title: Stream Docker daemon events
commands.useCase: Watch real-time daemon-level events like container start, stop, die, and OOM across the whole host.

commands.title: Run a command in a running container

flags.description: Allocate an interactive TTY for a shell session

commands.useCase: Get a shell inside a running container to inspect files, test connectivity, or run diagnostic commands.

commands.title: Attach to a running container's main process
commands.useCase: Attach directly to the container's PID 1 stdin/stdout, useful for interacting with a foreground process.
commands.beginnerNote: Detaching with Ctrl-C often kills the container's main process. Use the Ctrl-P Ctrl-Q escape sequence to detach without stopping it.

commands.title: Copy a file into a container
commands.useCase: Push a config file or patched asset into a running container without rebuilding the image.

commands.title: Copy a file out of a container
commands.useCase: Pull a log file or generated report out of a container for local inspection.

commands.title: Show filesystem changes in a container
commands.useCase: See exactly which files were added, changed, or deleted inside a container compared to its base image.

commands.title: Create a network
commands.useCase: Create a named network so containers can reach each other by service name instead of IP address, which changes on every restart.

commands.title: List networks
commands.useCase: See what networks exist before deciding whether to create a new one or reuse an existing one.

commands.title: Inspect a network
commands.useCase: Check which containers are attached to a network and what IPs they were assigned, when containers can't reach each other.

commands.title: Connect a container to a network
commands.useCase: Attach an already-running container to an additional network without recreating it.

commands.title: Remove a network
commands.useCase: Clean up an unused custom network left over from a stopped project.
commands.beginnerNote: Fails if any container is still attached to the network — disconnect or remove those containers first.

commands.title: Create a volume
commands.useCase: Create a named, persistent volume for a database container so data survives container recreation.

commands.title: List volumes
commands.useCase: See what named volumes exist on the host, including ones left behind by containers that have since been removed.

commands.title: Inspect a volume
commands.useCase: Find the actual host filesystem path backing a named volume for direct inspection or backup.

commands.title: Remove a volume
commands.useCase: Permanently delete a named volume and all the data stored in it once it's no longer needed.
commands.beginnerNote: This permanently deletes all data in the volume with no undo. Double check nothing still depends on it before running this against a shared environment.

commands.title: Remove unused volumes
commands.useCase: Reclaim disk space taken up by volumes left behind from containers that were removed without --volumes.
commands.beginnerNote: Removes all volumes not attached to any container, including volumes with data you might want. There is no undo — check docker volume ls first.

commands.title: Start services defined in a compose file

flags.description: Run all services detached in the background
Rebuild images before starting, instead of using cached ones

commands.useCase: Bring up an entire multi-container stack, like an app plus its database and cache, with one command.
commands.beginnerNote: depends_on only waits for a container to start, not for the service inside it to be ready. A database container starting does not mean PostgreSQL is accepting connections yet — use healthchecks for real readiness waiting.

commands.title: Stop and remove compose services

flags.description: Also remove the named volumes declared in the compose file

commands.useCase: Tear down an entire stack — containers, networks, and optionally volumes — after finishing local development.
commands.beginnerNote: Without -v your named volumes persist, which is usually what you want for databases, but it surprises people expecting a completely clean slate.

commands.title: Build or rebuild compose services
commands.useCase: Rebuild service images after changing a Dockerfile without also restarting the containers.

commands.title: View logs from compose services

flags.description: Stream logs continuously from all services

commands.useCase: Watch combined, colour-coded logs from every service in a stack at once while debugging a startup issue.

commands.title: List compose services
commands.useCase: Check the status of every service defined in the current compose file.

commands.title: Run a command in a compose service
commands.useCase: Open a shell inside one specific service from a multi-container compose stack, by service name rather than container ID.

commands.title: Restart compose services
commands.useCase: Restart every service in a stack, or a single named service, to pick up a config or environment change.

commands.title: Clean up unused Docker data

flags.description: Also remove all unused images, not just dangling ones
Also remove unused volumes

commands.useCase: Reclaim significant disk space when a build server or laptop is running low on space from accumulated Docker artifacts.
commands.beginnerNote: Removes ALL stopped containers, unused networks, dangling images, and build cache with no undo. On a CI server this is safe to run regularly, but on a development machine it removes months of cached layers and will slow your next build significantly.

commands.title: Show Docker disk usage
commands.useCase: Check how much disk space images, containers, volumes, and build cache are consuming before deciding whether to prune.

commands.title: Show Docker system-wide info
commands.useCase: Check the storage driver, number of running containers, and daemon configuration when debugging an environment issue.

commands.title: Show Docker version info
commands.useCase: Confirm client and server (daemon) version before troubleshooting an API compatibility issue.

commands.title: Log in to a registry
commands.useCase: Authenticate against a private or third-party registry before pulling or pushing images that require credentials.
commands.beginnerNote: Credentials are stored in plaintext in ~/.docker/config.json by default unless a credential helper is configured — worth knowing on a shared machine.

commands.title: Save an image to a tar archive
commands.useCase: Export an image to a file for transfer to an air-gapped machine with no registry access.

commands.title: Load an image from a tar archive
commands.useCase: Import an image on a machine that received it via docker save instead of pulling from a registry.
